text: The surgeon begins the procedure on the left side of the abdomen, initiating the dissection process.
The left ureter is visible as the surgeon employs a lateral to medial approach during the open colectomy procedure.
The surgeon approaches the descending colon, moving proximally towards the splenic flexure, using a technique that avoids taking vessels intercorporeally.

The surgeon demonstrates two techniques for mobilizing the splenic flexure: detaching the omentum from the distal transverse colon and splenic flexure in slimmer patients, or accessing the lesser sac and removing the omentum along with the splenic flexure in heavier patients.
After fully mobilizing the splenic flexure, the surgeon shifts focus to the right colon, visually identifying the right ureter.
The surgeon performs a lateral to medial dissection, scoring the peritoneum around the base of the cecum and terminal ileum to access the retroperitoneal plane.
The surgeon uses a BOVI electrocautery device to perform dissection, creating a clean plane without tearing or pulling on the tissues.
The surgeon gently sweeps the filmy tissues along the right lateral peritoneal reflection to expose the correct dissection plane.
The surgeon gradually moves the dissection cephalad and medially, exposing the entire right retroperitoneum during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter, duodenum, and inferior vena cava while approaching the hepatic flexure. The dissection plane shifts medially as the surgeon progresses towards the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon carefully avoids dissection near the right kidney while mobilizing the right colon. In the right side up Trendelenburg position, the medial attachments of the terminal ileum are dissected up to the duodenum to achieve maximum mobility for the creation of the ilioanal pouch.
The surgeon elevates and divides the hepatocolic attachments near the hepatic flexure, completing the dissection of the retroperitoneum beneath these structures.
The hepatic flexure is positioned below the level of the umbilicus, indicating that the right colon is adequately mobilized for extraction. The surgeon then begins dissection in the pelvic region.

The surgeon opens the dissection plane, revealing the left ureter in the left lower quadrant of the visual field. The left pararectal tissues are scored to gain entry into the presacral space. The rectum is then elevated using cautery, maintaining a bloodless plane.

The surgeon uses simple cautery to maintain the correct dissection plane, applying tension on the rectum. The dissection proceeds along the left lateral pararectal attachments before moving to the right side of the rectum.

The right ureter is visible in the right lower quadrant of the surgical field. The surgeon scores the right pararectal tissues to enter the presacral space, where previous dissection has been completed from the left side.
The surgeon applies tension to the right pararectal tissues and continues the dissection in the presacral space, maintaining the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon carefully delineates the posterior wall of the vagina, ensuring not to damage it, especially in patients with a rectocele. After completing the posterior and bilateral dissection, tension is placed anteriorly to facilitate the completion of the dissection.

The surgeon uses a sizer or rigid proctoscope in the rectum to apply traction and delineate the tissue planes. A stapler is utilized through the right lower quadrant port to assist in this challenging part of the procedure.
The surgeon applies significant traction on the rectum with assistance from the perineum, causing a rim of tissue to retract into the pelvic floor when released.

The surgeon applies multiple staples, creating a visible staple line that cones down into the anal canal. The staple line is positioned at the top of the anal canal, leaving only one to two centimeters of mucosa in the proximal half.

The surgeon exteriorizes the colon and rectum through a peri-umbilical incision, positioning them as midline structures. The pouch is then created by utilizing the base of the small bowel mesentery beneath the incision, ensuring adequate length for the pouch construction.
The surgeon exteriorizes the entire small bowel to visually inspect for any signs of Crohn's disease in a patient with presumed ulcerative colitis.

The final incisions include a peri-umbilical extraction site and two five-millimeter ports at the ileostomy site. A suprapubic port is used to bring out a drain, resulting in a cosmetic appearance post-ileostomy closure.
The surgeon demonstrates the extraction of a bulky rectum, affected by disease and inflammation, through a standard-sized ileostomy site.
The surgeon creates a J-pouch in standard fashion, ensuring it extends down to the pubis.

The surgeon brings up the ileostomy and places a clamp across the small bowel. The pneumoperitoneum is reestablished, revealing the staple line positioned well. The surgeon checks the cut edge of the small bowel mesentery for twists by tracing it up to the duodenum and ensures no loops of small bowel have slipped beneath the mesentery. The anastomosis is then created.
